content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 8, 2009

Thomas W. Hiltachk
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-213

Dear Mr. Hiltachk:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Kevin Johnson, the
Mayor of the City of Sacramento, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that
may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to
prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political
Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)²

Finally, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of conflict-of-interest laws outside the Act
such as common law conflict of interest.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulations may be found in Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² We note that, on August 27, 2009, we received a separate request for advice on the same issues on behalf
of the entire Sacramento City Council (including Mayor Johnson) from Eileen Teichert, the Sacramento City
Attorney. We are issuing a separate informal advice letter to Ms. Teichert (see Teichert Advice Letter, No. I-09-
207) on the issue of whether Mayor Johnson if he were to make a future loan to the Sacramentans for Accountable
Government committee, would be permitted to participate in City Council decisions on the issues discussed below.
We view the essential facts provided in Ms. Teichert's request as supplemental to those provided in your request
and, therefore, our advice herein is based on the facts in both your advice request and Ms. Teichert's.

Our File No. A-09-213
Page 2

QUESTION

Does Mayor Kevin Johnson have a conflict of interest that would disqualify him from
participating in decisions that would have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on
Sacramentans for Accountable Government?

CONCLUSION

No. Your account of the facts indicates that Mayor Johnson does not have an economic
interest in Sacramentans for Accountable Government and, assuming this to be true, he will not
have a disqualifying conflict of interest in governmental decisions affecting Sacramentans for
Accountable Government.

FACTS

The salient facts, as we understand them, are as follows. Sacramento's current charter
established a Council Manager form of government, with a nine member City Council composed
of the Mayor and eight councilmembers. The Mayor is campaigning for an initiative measure
that would amend Sacramento's charter, changing its form of government to a "Mayor-Council"
model. On June 4, 2009, the Mayor loaned $25,000 to Sacramentans for Accountable
Government ("SAG"), a recipient committee primarily formed to qualify and support, among
other things, the initiative measure, known as "The Strong Mayor Initiative." In the same month,
"The Strong Mayor Initiative" qualified for the ballot, requiring the City Council to place it
before the voters on a future ballot. On August 6, 2009 the City Council voted to put the
measure to a vote on June 8, 2010.

On August 25, 2009, the Mayor forgave the loan to SAG, converting what had been a
loan to a contribution. The City Council had on its agenda for August 25 an item relating to
possible flaws in the measure, and potential curative actions that the City Council might take, as
outlined in a "Legal Issues & Options Report." Prior to this meeting a member of the public and
a councilmember suggested that the Mayor might have an economic interest in SAG that could
create a conflict of interest disqualifying the Mayor from participation in decisions that would
have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on SAG. The City Attorney advised that
the Mayor might indeed have a conflict of interest, but the Mayor disagreed. When the Mayor
declined to recuse himself as advised by the City Attorney, the City Council tabled this agenda
item, which will be taken up at a later date. The City Council then instructed the City Attorney
to seek advice from the Commission as to whether or not Mayor Johnson has a disqualifying
conflict of interest in decisions relating to the Report described above.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions are designed to ensure that public officials will
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,

Our File No. A-09-213
Page 3


Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision when it is
"reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of
the official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has
adopted an eight-step analytical framework to determine whether a public official has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in a particular governmental decision, which we apply below.
(See Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step One: Is the Mayor a Public Official?

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency." (Section 82048, Regulation 18701.) The Mayor is clearly a public official
within the meaning of the Act.³

Step Two: Will the Mayor, Sitting as a Member of the City Council, be Making,
Participating in Making, or Using His Position to Influence a Governmental Decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency
to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.1.)

A public official "participates in making" a governmental decision when he or she,
without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations on a decision.
(Regulation 18702.2.)⁴ A public official is using his or her official position to "influence a
governmental decision" if he or she contacts or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee, or consultant of the City regarding the decisions.
(Regulation 18702.3.)

_______________
³ If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 files" include city council members) and
he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately,
prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest; as discussed in Regulation 18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2)
recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For
closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in Regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

⁴ Exception: making or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a public
official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function to
represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's wholly owned real property or business entity.
(See Regulation 18702.4.)

Our File No. A-09-213
Page 4

By voting on or otherwise participating in upcoming City Council decisions regarding options outlined in the Legal Issues & Options Report or decisions such as designating councilmembers to author statements supporting or opposing the initiative or approving the City Attorney's legal analysis of issues raised in this process, the Mayor would be making or participating in a governmental decision.

Step Three: Identifying the Mayor's Economic Interests—Possible Sources of Conflicts of Interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable the decision will have material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, (Section 87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5) as follows:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or if he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5).

Because you have not provided any facts concerning other possible economic interests of the Mayor, our analysis is limited to economic interests similar to those the Mayor may have had in or on account of his association with SAG by virtue of the loan he made to SAG.

The facts indicate the Mayor does not control SAG and is not a principal officer of the committee and therefore has no potential personal liability for committee debts.³ Nevertheless,

³ We do not in this letter offer an analysis or opinion of whether a candidate, committee treasurer or committee principal officer is personally liable for the debts of the committee.

Our File No. A-09-213
Page 5


an official may still have an economic interest in a committee when, for example, he or she has
assumed personal liability for the debts of the committee, receives gifts or income from it, or the
effects of governmental decisions on the committee impact the official's personal finances.

In the event that SAG received a loan from the Mayor for $25,000 and remained
obligated to repay the loan, there would be two economic interests potentially at issue for the
Mayor: his interest in SAG as a source of income to him and the Mayor's interest in his personal
finances.

Source of Income. If SAG has not made payments of $500 or more to the Mayor on the
loan, it would not yet be an actual source of income to the Mayor for purposes of the Act's
conflict-of-interest provisions. (See Section 87103(c).) However, as stated above, Section
87103(c) provides that an individual or entity is a source of income to an official for purposes of
the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions if, among other things, the individual or entity promises
income of $500 or more to the official during the 12-month period before the official participates
in a governmental decision having a financial effect on the source of income. Regulation
18703.3(a) elaborates on this concept, stating: "A public official's income includes income which
has been promised to the public official but not yet received by him or her, if he or she has a
legally enforceable right to the promised income." Therefore, assuming the Mayor had an
enforceable right to repayment of the loan to SAG, SAG would remain a source of promised
income to the Mayor as long as the loan remained a loan, with a balance at $500 or more.

Personal Financial Effect. If SAG owed the Mayor on a loan, any governmental decision
having a reasonably foreseeable effect on repayment of the loan would have a personal financial
effect on the Mayor.⁶ As stated in Regulation 18703.5, a governmental decision will have a
personal financial effect "if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing."

Your account of the facts indicates that on August 25, 2009, Mayor Johnson forgave the
loan he made to SAG, eliminating any legal obligation on behalf of SAG to repay the loan.
Furthermore, your account of the facts does not suggest that SAG had begun making payments
prior to the Mayor's forgiveness of the loan on August 25, 2009, or that the Mayor has otherwise
received $500 or more in income from SAG within the prior 12 months. Assuming this to be
correct, the committee is not a "source of income" to the Mayor.

Nor do the facts before us suggest any connection between SAG and the Mayor's
personal finances. In particular, we are aware of no effect of a decision relating to SAG that
could have any effect on the Mayor's personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities. We
conclude that the Mayor has no economic interest in SAG, and therefore he will not have a
disqualifying conflict of interest in the governmental decisions you describe relative to
Sacramentans for Accountable Government.


⁶ See the Teichert Advice Letter, No. I-09-207, for this analysis.

Our File No. A-09-213
Page 6


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

participating in decisions that would have a reasonably foreseeable, material iinancial effect on
Sacramentans for Accountable Government?

CONCLUSION: No. Your account of the facts indicates that Mayor Johnson does not have an economic
interest in Sacramentans for Accountable Government and, assuming this to be true, he will not
have a disqualifying conflict of interest in govemmental decisions affecting Sacramentans for
Accountable Government.
